item.title: Lessons of Darkness, 1992 - ★★★★★
item.description:
ty Claude:

How much oil has humanity used?

Commercial oil extraction only began around 165 years ago — so we’re really talking about industrial-era consumption. A total of roughly 1,572 billion barrels of crude oil were produced historically from 1900 through 2024. To put that in perspective, the world currently consumes around 37.4 billion barrels per year, or about 102.5 million barrels per day. 

How much is left?

This depends heavily on how you define “reserves”:
 ∙ Proven reserves (high confidence, economically extractable today): As of year-end 2024, the world’s total proven crude oil reserves stood at approximately 1,567 billion barrels — enough to meet current global demand for roughly 47 years at today’s consumption rate.
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker, 2019
item.letterboxd:filmTitle: Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker
item.description:
As an ally of adoption, I support the ending of this movie.

Sheev Palpatine, the greatest project manager of his generation?
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Star Wars: The Last Jedi, 2017 - ★★★
item.description:
The story is a bold choice, can't argue that. And on paper there is a lot to love. But when I watch it, no matter how hard I try, I just can never get behind this spiteful, grating Luke Skywalker. Yes, someone at the end of their life may still need to learn simple truths and continue to grow, it doesn't mean I have to like how it's portrayed.

The Great Fuel Chase™ will never not be dumb, and I'll never understand how this gets put in a Star Wars movie. But we got some of the best scenes ever with Kylo and Rey, and for that I say thank you Rian. Thank you, for everything.
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Star Wars: The Force Awakens, 2015 - ★★★★★
item.description:
Incredible pacing, whole movie cooks nonstop and looks so good.

They had to thread the needle creating new Star Wars characters who fit a universe that requires campy antics, endless charisma, and emotional resonance and they absolutely nail it with Adam Driver, John Boyega, and Daisy Ridley.

I definitely am on the side of pro remake. If you make a good movie, it's a good movie! We will always have remakes, and to me that's fine if they are done well. They can even be interesting in their own way with how you can spin elements to be slightly different, giving a new perspective on the story as a whole. The familial element makes the parallels for Kylo compelling with his uncle and grandfather. And Han filling the role of Obi-wan, committing to self-sacrifice in order to save those you love, is a wonderful twist.

These movies get ragged on for being three separate entities, but with this viewing I found a lot of connective tissue between the trilogy and for that I am grateful. JJ, thank you. for everything.
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: True Romance, 1993
item.description:
Having watched all the films Quentin Tarantino has directed, watching this script he wrote between Reservoir Dogs and Pulp Fiction is like a time machine. It's through and through a Tarantino picture but get this... directed by Tony Scott. Juice Master many have called him. And he is squirting here.

My brain fell out as the cast was listed during the opening credits. Just name after name, but truly this is Patricia Arquette's movie. My god she is so good.

Yes it is full of all the Tarantino tropes, and probably the thickest they have ever been, but boy what a ride. What a time.

withholding stars for the eventual 70mm episode (TBD)
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: The Web, 2013
item.description: Watched on Monday March 2, 2026.
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Wuthering Heights, 2026 - ★★★
item.description:
Emerald Fennell has great style, and will be watching her career with great interest. Her vision, especially for the home of Wuthering Heights and the surrounding landscape was wonderful. Very strong, forceful direction that I want to see; I kept thinking of David Lowery and what he did with The Green Knight. Such striking imagery, and I believe it's only a matter of time until EF has a movie that blows the general public's socks off.

I don't have any qualms with an adaptation this loose, and I wonder if there would be less issue if it was in a different time period like Romeo + Juliet. But I did not fully buy into the love story in the way that it was presented. Jenna and I talked about this afterward, and my issue was not believing the deep, longing, understanding love that was presented in the film because the way that it was shown was primarily through their physical relationship. Which you could argue was an effect of their love, thus proving it. But I wanted more from an emotional, spiritual level that I think the movie believed that it established. More depth of conversation would have gone a long way for me.

Jenna's counterpoint to this was the "trauma bond" that is established when you see them as children, and that as a viewer you can fill in the gaps because those moments were so visceral. I think that is a strong reading and not something I acknowledged while watching.

I did not love Elordi as Heathcliff. Is his voice just too deep? No X factor chemistry from him and Robbie, unfortunately. But I did keep thinking what a disaster that Elordi was not cast as one of the Beatles. He would make a great Paul.

Outside of that, great casting and performances from everyone. Alison Oliver was a delight. How about a movie with three women who all had depth and compelling backstories and satisfying arcs?
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Willow, 1988 - ★★★★★
item.description: Movie magic. Val Kilmer and Joanne Whalley, it's like watching mom and dad fall in love. Love the score. Was in a fever fugue state in and out of consciousness and waking up to Warwick Davis running around with a baby was just what I wanted.
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Hulk, 2003 - ★★★★★
item.description:
Incredibly refreshing when you have been stranded in the Marvel desert for decades. True auteurs if you ask me. they built this entire lab in real life so that they could destroy it. They don't make movies like that anymore. Look it up, it's true.

Jennifer Connelly is so beautiful I could die. Nick Nolte is so courageous I want to keep living. The Absorbing Man. On 70mm we were gushing over the effect of his arm melding with the chair. Retro oscar for special effects now.

It's like a cartoon Shakespeare tragedy and no one has dared to attempt something like it since. It has a soul which you cannot buy with money, which is why Marvel movies don't have them.
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Taylor Tomlinson: Prodigal Daughter, 2026 - ★★★
item.description: Amazing location. All the religious stuff gets a long in the tooth
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Empire of the Dark, 1990 - ★★★★
item.description:
All the heart you can hope for in a b movie. Editing and production clearly lacking but punching up in some other categories? Loved the music and the opening credits, the dummy work was fantastic and Steve Barkett’s wardrobe delivering. Total weekend dad energy the whole time.

Loved the Cobra homage with the grocery store shootout. This guy wasting multiple customers for that can of tuna.

The final action scene goes on and on but I can’t blame them for showing the same decapitation 3 times, I laughed all the same.
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Heat Vision and Jack, 1999
item.description: An artifact from a time that was hardly real.
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Flipped, 2010
item.description: The constant light bloom is how you know it’s set in 1963
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Best in Show, 2000 - ★★★
item.description:
Eugene Levy having 2 left feet got things thing off on the right foot. Him and Catherine O'Hara are very good together, and I love Christopher Guest's character. So similar to Waiting for Guffman, I guess all these movies are the same? "not broke" etc.

Definitely enjoyed this more than WfG. Didn't really laugh out loud. 2 chuckles, 10 smiles, 1 snort, 3 giggles. i'm a tuff pistachio nut to crack I guess
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: The Hobbit, 1977 - ★★★
item.description: Beautiful backgrounds. So many great creature designs. Really love the wood elves being more like goblins. Smaug covered in hair is awesome.
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Galaxy Quest, 1999
item.description: I feel like I could quote every line
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: My Own Private Idaho, 1991
item.description:
Scott and Mike having inverted lives while living parallel to eachother is compelling and makes for some beautiful and striking moments. The Shakespeare of it all felt unnecessary and while it added to the dreamlike qualities it is mostly distracting. 

I love the story but did not love the execution.
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Sinners, 2025 - ★★★
item.description:
Now we are talking!
(No seriously the movie stops for 30 minutes for a conversation at a doorway)
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Boyz n the Hood, 1991 - ★★★★★
item.description:
John Singleton lived the dream of any young director. Write a semi biographical story in high school, sell it to a studio, direct it yourself, and receive Oscar. What a ride it must have been!

So many powerful moments, I keep seeing the tear roll down Tre’s cheek when he is with the cops.
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Clearcut, 1991 - ★★★★
item.description:
Some wrongs, no amount of justice can make right. Some men of misdeeds will never suffer vengeance or pay the price. For those who are the victim, acts of vigilantism may only compound suffering and weaken their case.

But perhaps an act of defiance to the powers that be can cause a ripple to a wave, or maybe just a roar of madness that is swallowed by the wilderness.
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Friday the 13th: A New Beginning, 1985 - ★★
item.description: Randy quaid origin story
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: GOAT, 2026 - ★★★★
item.description: Adorable and so funny, my whole family cracking up, looking at eachother in the movie theater like Ray Liotta in goodfellas. Comedy of the year rn. Took a mental snapshot of Alby and his buddy Shea hugging in the aisle at the end. What dreams may come for these ballers for real
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Waiting for Guffman, 1996 - ★★
item.description: Almost funny
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Black Dynamite, 2009 - ★★★★
item.description: This will age beautifully with each viewing
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Spider-Man: Into the Spider-Verse, 2018 - ★★★★★
item.description: Watched on Monday February 9, 2026.
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Exotica, 1994 - ★
item.description:
The score is very good, but that's about all I liked. Two thirds I almost DNFed but my curiosity got the better of me. Basically a mystery until the final 15 minutes and the reveal is an absolute dud.

It's easy to take good writing, dialogue and acting for granted until you are confronted by the alternative. Mia Kirshner was the only bright spot. Also I know it's 1994 but how are you only charging $5 a dance?
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Demolition Man, 1993 - ★★★
item.description: Felt like it would never end
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Boiler Room, 2000 - ★★★★★
item.description: Giovanni Oscar when
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: The Fugitive, 1993 - ★★★★★
item.description:
Tommy Lee Jones face the best to ever do it.

Highly recommend watching this with 7 of your best friends in a mountain house while a blizzard with 45 mph winds come through.
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: The Patriot, 2000 - ★★★★
item.description: Mel Gibson’s crying choking throwing up face, no one does it better.
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Lethal Weapon, 1987 - ★★★★
item.description: After killing a man Murtaugh goes home to a few beers and works on his boat. All in a days work
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Payback, 1999 - ★★★★
item.description: Noir for days. Thank you helgeland
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Tequila Sunrise, 1988 - ★★★★
item.description: Didn’t understand a lick of the story but it was gorgeous and filled with beautiful people.
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Disneyland Handcrafted, 2026
item.description:
Stunning footage from 1955, I could have watched raw footage of them working all day. The timeline to accomplish Disneyland, my mind cannot comprehend how they did this. It was a dirt field 10 months before opening!

There is literally a million details that needed to be handled, and someone says at one point they had no project managers…? The craftsmanship and expertise, collaboration and will to complete what has never been done before is inspiring.

Walt Disney says at one point something like I never wanted to have a big pile of money, that’s not what it’s for, I want to make something with it. Thank you Walt for making things.
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Lars and the Real Girl, 2007 - ★★★
item.description:
When this came out in 2007 I was in a depressed state. My high school friends had moved out of state and I was treading water at community college. I had a crush who I was spending time with. We would hang out at her house, or go into the city, but it was clear that I didn't have a chance.

When she told me that she had seen an indie movie called Lars and the Real Girl with friends and the main character reminded her of me, I had to watch the trailer. I reminded her of a guy who couldn't be touched and was in a long term relationship with a life sized doll.

Friends and family had always said that I was a little different, "peculiar". But when this girl and her family who I had only met within the past year all agreed that I was like Lars, it hit different and always stayed with me. I wasn't Lars just in the community I grew up with, I was like that to the wider world.

I have carried that comparison with me for nearly 20 years, knowing that someday I would watch this and get the full dose of what they meant. I have come a long way since 2007, and I know that part of what they said is still true.

Ryan Gosling is so great. Before he became the silent stoic heartthrob, he was Lars. I love the message of this movie. Something someone is doing might make no sense to you, but they don't need judgment. They just need time and to be met where they are at.

🥀 Sad white bois forever 🥀
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Shampoo, 1975 - ★★
item.description:
Warren Beatty wrote a script about a guy (played by him) porking a handful of women over the course of a day. Other than that, nothing really happens. His hair is nightmare fuel.

Jenna stayed awake for 10 minutes.
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Black Panther, 2018
item.description: When Bilbo interrogates Gollum, wow
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Blazing Saddles, 1974
item.description: A definitive story about race in America and the musical fruit.
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Jackass: The Movie, 2002 - ★★★
item.description:
My first time seeing this gooch to shaved cowlick, but you could not escape most of these antics in the 2000s.

Kids these days don't even know, watching someone on youtube dribbling a basketball in a target until they get asked to leave thinking they are a menace. you don't even know.

could have used less man flesh and spittle but say la vee
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Once Upon a Time in the West, 1968 - ★★★★
item.letterboxd:filmTitle: Once Upon a Time in the West
item.description:
Another beautiful technicolor 4K transfer from Sergio Leone and Tonino Delli Colli. Blown away by the sets and locations.

Did not quite reach the levels of The Good, the Bad and the Ugly, but the final act does a lot of lifting to solidify it as an iconic revenge story.

I enjoyed the portrayal of Jill by Claudia Cardinale. If that happened to me I would be out of this town, but her resilience and determination was inspiring.
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: The Dark Knight, 2008 - ★★★★
item.description:
For a few brief moments, this is the best Batman.

Winnie and Theo watched the last hour with me. At every "iconic" moment I turned and looked at them deadpan to see their reaction. They just stared back at me, dolls eyes. We were all doing the batman voice by the end. How can you not?
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Batman Forever, 1995 - ★★★
item.description: For a few brief moments, this is the best Batman.
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: How to Train Your Dragon, 2025
item.description:
I told my boys that this was streaming and we could watch it now. But Theo said he wanted to watch the animated instead. After a bit of arguing, they agreed to give this a try since it was "new" and we had seen the animated version before.

Let's just say after 15 minutes we switched back to the animated.
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Bad Lieutenant, 1992 - ★★★
item.description:
~ * ~ * Lover's Journey AKA Hater Movie Challenge 2026 * ~ * ~

It's kismet that I watched this the day after Marty Supreme which featured Abel Ferrara. But also, you can see how influenced the Safdie's have been by his movies. If you liked the chaos of Howard Ratner in Uncut Gems, well I've got a movie for you.

I did not find this as complete a film as Ms. 45 or The Addiction but Harvey Keitel puts on quite a performance. Seriously, this guy is bad.
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Marty Supreme, 2025 - ★★★★★
item.description:
During the infamous “honey” scene Jenna leaned over to me and whispered “Josh Shafty is kind of a freak”. Indeed.

Ping pong ain’t that serious. At face value, it’s just not as serious as say, dying for your country and your family. But ping pong, and anything you want in life, can be that serious for you. It can be the thing that keeps your brain strapped between your ears as you hurl into oblivion.

At one point Marty says he has meaning where others do not. Things happen to other people, but Marty makes what needs to happen to achieve his goals. It’s inspiring when you see a good person achieve their dream through hard work and determination. When an uncouth weasel leaves a path of wreckage, it’s cinema.

Loved the ending so much, saying the right amount about Marty. For those who have shared that moment, it felt like a special message for how life is a matter of perspective, and what you thought you knew about yourself is just the chapter of your life you are currently living.
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Decision to Leave, 2022 - ★★★
item.description:
Park Chan-wook’s ASMR streak continues. I don’t know what to make of this. I enjoyed the mystery of the first half and what it was building towards, but the 2nd half and resolution completely lost me. No idea what the ending was about. Am I stupid? Another unsolved mystery.

I love the goofiness and humor, but it does feel incongruent to the overall tone of the film. Slappy spinoff tv series when
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Bugonia, 2025 - ★★★★
item.description:
Delightfully entertaining. Lanthimos weirdness is present but much more constrained in story written by someone else, truly a blessing.

While watching I was reminded of Signs in the way the characters felt isolated in a house, the searching for truth, the unresolved past.

So much is packed into the characters, particularly Jesse Plemons, in a way that allows the story to be a quirky hoot on the surface but underneath bear the weight of fears and past scars that can slowly choke you to death over the course of a lifetime.

There is paranoia and a truth that the story has to reconcile by the end. It could have played out in many different ways, and this is a rare occurrence where I would have been happy with any of them because the lead up was so satisfying. It almost didn't matter how it ended because the journey was so good.
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Under the Silver Lake, 2018 - ★★
item.description:
It was painful having to look at Andrew Garfield’s unwashed hair for 2 hours.

40 minute epilogue a catastrophic mistake. You can see a pattern in directors who will, having achieved personal success and are allowed to take a risk, indulging in their love of cinema and film history. Chock full of references to Hitchcock (a literal epitaph, DRM say it ain’t so!), film posters, camera flourishes, homage… it’s overwhelming and seemingly unguided. All I can say is he likes movies a lot and wants you to know. A damning conclusion to arrive at.

Still, I had a fun time overall. A smarmy creep moping about L.A. trying to solve conspiracy theories suits me nicely. Tonally there is something Lynchian that works well with the subject matter. But the rabbit holes kept multiplying until it runs out of gas.

Without a satisfying end to the story, I think it is successful in giving a conspiracy theorist their worst nightmare: what if all of your conspiracies were true?
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: (There Is No) Cure, 2022
item.description:
Really cool references to other films that establish tone and mood.

Tone is an attitude. a spell. the vibe that draws us in that we just can't shake off. it is the character of the film. it's personality. the impression that lingers when the end credits roll. the indelible brand it leaves on your psyche, your soul, when the lights come up. tone encompasses and ultimately defines a film. tone is a mask.
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: The Super Mario Bros. Movie, 2023 - ★★★
item.description: the excitement for the 2026 release begins. me and alby, he sat with me the whole time. I dozed off a few times, he stayed locked in. what it's all about
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: Haters Club Movie Challenge 2026
item.description:

item.description:
My fave watches of any year for the first time of 2026

  • Train Dreams

da ones we need on da shelves

  2. Mulholland Drive

item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: People as animals / subhuman
item.description:
I can't stand depictions of people as animals or some lesser form of human. Therefore I shall capture them in a list.

  • The Island of Dr. Moreau
  • Harry and the Hendersons
  • Altered States
  • George of the Jungle
  • The Jungle Book
  • Tarzan
  • The Fly
  • Babes in Toyland
  • Encino Man
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.description:

  • Sicario

  • Oblivion

item.title: 2024 top first time watches

  • Wicked
  • Tampopo

  • Kingdom of the Planet of the Apes

item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: The Criterion Challenge 2025
item.description:
Welcome to the fifth annual Criterion Challenge where we celebrate The Criterion Collection! Can you believe it has been HALF A DECADE? When I started this challenge, I never expected it to hit the ground running as fast as it did, so thanks to all of you for participating. I wanted this year to mix in some categories throughout the years as well as be even more collaborative. I took many of your suggestions and turned them into categories. It’s a very exciting line-up for 2025!

But first, time for the rules:

There are 52 categories. The goal is to watch any Criterion released film based on the categories below between 1/1/25-12/31/25. Your choices can be any films released by Criterion on 4K, Blu-ray, DVD, VHS, Laserdisc, or ones that have been featured on The Criterion Channel streaming service (although I'd prefer you choose films they've actually put out themselves).
The films can be watched daily, weekly, or monthly, and in any order! Watch them at your own pace, but remember you have to finish by 12/31/25.
I would like for all picks to be first time watches, but will leave that up to you.

Don't forget to visit The Criterion Collection for all your film needs, and they have a pretty sweet streaming service for endless options to choose from when you're struggling to find things to watch.

HUGE SHOUTOUT to Josh and Criterion Closet Picks for their amazing lists which helps this process be much smoother.

Now...what you all have been waiting for...

Categories:

1. Watch a film from the CC40 Boxset
2. Watch a film from the year you were born
3. Directed by Robert Altman
4. Watch a film that would be your first choice in the Criterion Closet
5. Great Soundtracks
6. John Turturro’s Adventures in Moviegoing
7. 1920s
8. Watch a film that will be added to the physical collection in 2025
9. 1930s
10. Andrew Garfield’s Closet Picks
11. 1940s
12. Celine Song’s Top 10
13. 1950s
14. Watch a film from the Criterion Channel’s all time favorites lists
15. 1960s
16. Watch a film that is currently out of print from the physical collection
17. 1970s
18. William Friedkin’s Closet Picks
19. 1980s
20. Spine #451-499
21. 1990s
22. Documentary
23. 2000s
24. Janus Contemporaries
25. 2010s
26. Bill Hader’s Second Closet Picks
27. 2020s
28. Noir and Neonoir
29. All Time Top Criterion Closet Picks
30. Criterion Releases Never Picked in the Closet
31. North American film
32. Ayo Edebiri’s Closet Picks
33. Wim Wenders’ Adventures in Moviegoing
34. South American film
35. Random Number Generator (Google random number generator, set values from 1 to whatever number Criterion has listed last here. This number will change as more releases are announced so please keep up to date by using the link I have provided as I will not be updating each time Criterion makes announcements. Watch whatever movie corresponds to the spine number you are given.)
36. AAPI Filmmakers
37. Watch a film shorter than 80 minutes
38. European film
39. Cult Movies
40. Isabella Rossellini’s Adventures in Moviegoing
41. Winona Ryder’s Closet Picks
42. African film
43. John Carpenter’s Top 10
44. Horror
45. Asian film
46. Dark Comedies
47. Rachel Kushner’s Adventures in Moviegoing
48. Australian film
49. Female Filmmakers
50. Ben Wheatley’s Closet Picks
51. A film by a director whose work you have not seen before
52. Watch any Criterion film from your watchlist

Helpful Links:
The Complete Criterion Collection
The Criterion Collection by Spine Number
Every Film Available on The Criterion Channel


Please use the tags "cc2025" and “criterion challenge 2025” so that we can keep track of everything, and link your lists in the comments below. Have fun!

Previous challenges:
The Criterion Challenge 2021
The Criterion Challenge 2022
The Criterion Challenge 2023
The Criterion Challenge 2024

Started:
Watched:
Completed:

  1. My Own Private Idaho
  2. Broadcast News
  3. 3 Women
  4. Samurai Rebellion
  5. Repo Man
  6. Wild Strawberries

item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: The Criterion Challenge 2024
item.description:
Fourth annual "The Criterion Challenge"

There are 52 categories. The goal is to watch any Criterion released film based on the categories below between 1/1/24-12/31/24.

Films must be released by Criterion on 4K, Blu-ray, DVD, VHS, Laserdisc, or been featured on The Criterion Channel streaming service.

Categories:
1. Spine #351-450
2. Spine #601-700
3. Watch a film with a rating of 4.3 or higher
4. First film in your watchlist
5. Most recently added film in your watchlist
6. Longest runtime in your watchlist
7. Most popular film in your watchlist
8. Martin Scorsese's World Cinema Project
9. Criterion Staff Picks
10. New York Film Festival Favorites
11. Watch a film in the Saturday Matinee series "Observations on Film Art"
12. First Films - watch a directorial debut
13. Virtually Reality
14. Random Number Generator (Google random number generator, set values from 1 to whatever number Criterion has listed last here. This number will change as more releases are announced so please keep up to date by using the link I have provided as I will not be updating each time Criterion makes announcements. Watch whatever movie corresponds to the spine number you are given.)
15. Technicolor
16. Women Auteurs
17. Criterion Sight and Sound
18. Dreaming of a Holiday Movie Marathon
19. Watch a Netflix Original released by Criterion
20. 100 Years of Olympic Films
21. LGBTQ+
22. Directed by Luis Buñuel
23. Directed by Mai Zetterling
24. Starring Liv Ullmann
25. Italian Neorealism
26. Hong Kong Cinema
27. Made in Brazil
28. Ari Aster’s Closet Picks
29. Yo La Tengo’s Closet Picks
30. Charlie Day’s Closet Picks
31. Michael Shannon’s Closet Picks
32. Aubrey Plaza’s Top 10
33. Anthony Bourdain’s Top 10
34. Alan Arkin’s Top 10
35. Tunde Adebimpe’s Top 10
36. Genre: Made for TV
37. Genre: Family
38. Genre: Animation
39. Genre: History
40. Guillermo del Toro’s Twitter Film recommendations
41. 1920s
42. 1930s
43. 1940s
44. 1950s
45. 1960s
46. 1970s
47. 1980s
48. 1990s
49. 2000s
50. 2010s
51. 2020s
52. Watch any Criterion film from your watchlist

Helpful Links:
The Complete Criterion Collection
The Criterion Collection by Spine Number
Every Film Available on The Criterion Channel

Started: January 1st
Completed:

  1. Le Bonheur
  2. Harold and Maude
  3. Woman in the Dunes
  4. Withnail & I
  5. The Magician
  6. A Brighter Summer Day
  7. Breathless
  8. Touki Bouki
  9. Police Story
  10. Beau Travail

...plus 42 more. View the full list on Letterboxd.

item.title: 1 of Everything
item.description:
A list of your favorite from popular series. Clone this list and choose your own favorites. This is YOUR favorites, not what you consider to be "best". Be brave!

side note: if you do not have a favorite/ haven't seen them put a movie with title that shows that (i.e. "No")

1. Star Wars
2. Star Trek
3. Alien
4. Planet of the Apes
5. Indiana Jones
6. Terminator
7. Pixar
8. Disney Animation Studios
9. Dreamworks Animation
10. Don Bluth Animated Films
11. Studio Ghibli
12. Mission Impossible
13. Middle Earth
14. Harry Potter
15. Batman
16. DC Cinematic Universe
17. Marvel Cinematic Universe
18. X-Men
19. Spider-Man
20. The Fast and the Furious
21. Pirates of the Caribbean
22. Back to the Future
23. Rocky
24. Twilight
25. James Bond

  1. The Empire Strikes Back

    Star Wars

  2. Star Trek: Generations

    Star Trek

  3. Alien
  4. Planet of the Apes

    Planet of the Apes

  5. Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade

    Indiana Jones

  6. Terminator 2: Judgment Day

    Terminator

  7. Up

    Pixar

  8. The Jungle Book

    Disney Animation Studios

  9. The Prince of Egypt

    Dreamworks Animation

  10. An American Tail

    Don Bluth Animated Films

...plus 15 more. View the full list on Letterboxd.

item.title: Giant Insects
item.description:
I love giant insects in movies so here is an ongoing list as I spot them. Let me know if there are any I should add to the list!

  • Nausicaä of the Valley of the Wind
  • Honey, I Shrunk the Kids
  • Starship Troopers
  • King Kong
  • Eight Legged Freaks
  • Mimic
item.dc:creator: protolexus

item.title: 10 Favorite Movies 201X
item.description:
Interstellar

Interstellar is my #1 all time film because it gives me an entirely unique feeling. The size and scope of existence, the nothingness of my own quantity and how it does not matter, and yet, because I am I, it means everything.

Mad Max: Fury Road

I smiled ear to ear while watching this film. This hits on something on the inside about what I love about watching films. The crazy, beautiful, exciting. What I want to experience when I see a film, this nails it.

Phantom Thread

When watching this I remember thinking that it is the objectively best film I have ever seen. PTA does things that no one else comes close to. The way he explores humanity and relationship.

Under the Skin

The cinematography blew me away. The story that is told visually had me enthralled from start to finish. Simply some of the most beautiful scenes I have ever witnessed in film.

The Tree of Life

It has been awhile since I have seen this, but the experience has stuck with me.

Her

A love story like none other.

Parasite

This could be in the list because of the proximity, but I was truly blown away with how displaced I felt watching this. Also one of my most favorite endings.

First Man

I love space and I love films about space. Ryan Gosling’s performance as Neil Armstrong was fantastic. This was the first film that I have seen that grappled with the feat of getting to the moon.

Roma
Birdman

  1. Interstellar
  2. Mad Max: Fury Road
  3. Phantom Thread
  4. Under the Skin
  5. The Tree of Life
  6. Her
  7. Parasite
  8. First Man
  9. Roma
  10. Birdman or (The Unexpected Virtue of Ignorance)
item.dc:creator: protolexus
